one_line_takeaway: The first industrial study of LLM-based false-alarm reduction shows that hybrid LLM+static-analysis methods (evaluated, not invented, here) can cheaply eliminate 94-98% of a proprietary SAT's memory-safety false positives at Tencent, but recall still falls short of enterprise safety thresholds and long-context/complex-constraint cases remain unsolved.
key_finding: Hybrid LLM+static-analysis methods, especially LLM4PFA (prior work, used here as a baseline), are the strongest: LLM4PFA reaches accuracy 0.93-0.94 across all four backbones and eliminates 94-98% of false positives (FPR_R) while maintaining high recall (e.g., Claude-Opus-4 backbone: recall 0.88, FPR_R 0.94). Hybrid methods beat prompt-strategy-only LLMs, which in turn beat basic prompting; few-shot was the best prompt strategy while CoT was the weakest (sometimes worse than basic). Traditional learning-based detectors perform poorly out-of-distribution (accuracy 0.36-0.44), and LineVul's apparent 0.76 accuracy is degenerate (it labels everything a false positive: recall 0.00, FPR_R 1.00). LLM methods are cheap: 2.1-109.5 s and $0.0011-$0.12 per alarm versus 10-20 min of manual review. Per-bug-type, methods do best on DBZ (simple patterns) and worst on NPD (complex conditional constraints).
core_contribution: The first industrial empirical study of LLM-based false-positive (false alarm) reduction for static analysis tools (SATs), conducted at Tencent. The authors' own contributions are: (1) an industrial investigation (developer interviews + real data analysis) documenting the prevalence and cost of SAT false positives in a large enterprise; (2) a real-world benchmark of 433 bug alarms (328 false positives, 105 real bugs) drawn from Tencent's proprietary SAT 'BkCheck' on its Advertising and Marketing Services (AMS) codebase, covering three memory-safety bug types (NPD, OOB, DBZ); and (3) an extensive comparative evaluation of a wide range of techniques on this benchmark: basic prompting, three prompting strategies (CoT, few-shot, bug-type augmentation), two hybrid LLM+static-analysis methods, and four traditional learning-based detectors, across four backbone LLMs, with both effectiveness and cost measured. IMPORTANT ATTRIBUTION NOTE: the two hybrid methods LLM4SA (Wen et al. [60]) and LLM4PFA (Du et al. [15]) are PRIOR WORK that this paper evaluates as baselines, NOT methods proposed by this paper. LLM4PFA is prior work by an author of this study (Du), but it is still a baseline here, not a new contribution. The paper's novelty is the industrial study/benchmark/comparison, not any new detection technique.
threat_model: This is a security-adjacent software-engineering study, not an adversarial-security paper; it does not posit an attacker or defend against one, so no attacker threat model applies. Its security relevance is that the SATs under study detect memory-safety vulnerabilities (Null Pointer Dereference, Out-of-Bounds, Divide-by-Zero) that are genuine security/reliability defects. The practical problem is false-alarm fatigue: enterprise SATs deliberately over-approximate (favoring recall/soundness) and thus emit >90% false positives, forcing 10-20 minutes of manual triage per alarm; this both wastes effort and risks real vulnerabilities being missed amid noise. The paper studies whether LLMs can automatically triage away false positives while preserving high recall so that true bugs are not discarded.
limitations: (1) Recall of even the best method (LLM4PFA) stays below the enterprise-expected 90% safety threshold, so LLMs can replace at most one manual review round, not eliminate manual validation. (2) LLMs fail on long code contexts (all-methods-failed cases had functions averaging 95.6 lines longer than the dataset average) and on complex cascaded constraints (those cases had ~21 more conditional statements than average); they also mishandle uncommon syntax, pointer manipulation, and deeply nested user-defined data structures. (3) Strong results partly reflect that BkCheck's warnings mostly involve short code fragments; effectiveness degrades when deeper interprocedural/cross-module reasoning is needed. (4) Scope is limited to three memory-safety bug types at one company (Tencent AMS), threatening generalizability. (5) The 433-alarm dataset cannot be released due to confidentiality (only a replication package of methods/results is available), limiting reproducibility. (6) RQ1 rests on only 5 interviewees. (7) Traditional baselines were applied without enterprise fine-tuning (none available), which is realistic but disadvantages them.

datasets_used: Tencent AMS BkCheck false-alarm benchmark: 433 alarms (328 false positives, 105 real bugs), Sept 2024-June 2025, three bug types
Per-type composition: NPD 136 (107 FP / 29 real), DBZ 158 (139 FP / 19 real), OOB 139 (82 FP / 57 real)
Referenced (as prior benchmarks, not used to evaluate here): Juliet, Devign, Gawk, BigVul (BigVul was LineVul's training set)
quantitative_results: Overall false-positive proportion in dataset = 76% (328/433); estimated >90% before context-based filtering
LLM4PFA accuracy 0.93-0.94 across all four backbones; eliminates 94-98% of false positives (FPR_R)
LLM4PFA + Claude-Opus-4: accuracy 0.93, precision 0.83, recall 0.88, F1 0.85, FPR_P 0.96, FPR_R 0.94
LLM4PFA + GPT-4o: accuracy 0.93, FPR_R 0.98; + Qwen-3-Coder: accuracy 0.94, FPR_R 0.98; + DeepSeek-R1: accuracy 0.93, recall 0.86, FPR_R 0.95
Best prompt strategy few-shot (e.g., Claude-Opus-4 accuracy 0.72); basic prompting weak (accuracy 0.40-0.55); CoT weakest
Traditional learning baselines: Devign 0.44, ReGVD 0.36 (recall 0.87 but FPR_R only 0.19), LLMAO 0.36, LineVul 0.76 (degenerate: recall 0.00, FPR_R 1.00)
Cost per alarm: time 2.1-109.5 s, money $0.0011-$0.12 (Claude-Opus-4 most expensive at $0.12; DeepSeek-R1 slowest at ~109.5 s); manual review = 10-20 min per alarm
Bad-case analysis: all-methods-failed cases averaged 95.6 lines longer functions and ~21 more conditional statements than dataset average
baselines_compared: Hybrid LLM+static-analysis (prior work, evaluated as baselines): LLM4SA (Wen et al.), LLM4PFA (Du et al.)
Prompting strategies: basic prompt, Chain-of-Thought, few-shot, bug-type augmentation
Traditional learning-based: Devign (GNN), ReGVD (GNN), LLMAO (fine-tuned CodeGen PLM), LineVul (fine-tuned CodeBERT PLM)
Backbone LLMs compared: GPT-4o, Claude-Opus-4, Qwen-3-Coder, DeepSeek-R1
Human baseline: manual two-round code review (10-20 min/alarm)
